caveats: a: OEWS is for NAICS 236200 rather than the six-digit industrial-building industry and is 2023 vintage.
a: The wage-weighted task exposure is a bounded analyst transformation, not an observed automation rate.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and was harmonized across industries, so it may not perfectly represent current industrial-project economics.
rho: RICS respondents are global and only 10% were from the Americas.
rho: AGC reports use or investment intent, not production-scale implementation or labor substitution.
rho: Industrial projects have unusually stringent safety, engineering, owner-specification, and change-control requirements.
e: Repeat-customer revenue share is not the same as the share of firms eligible under the lens.
e: The contractor survey is Ontario-based and includes trade contractors as well as general contractors.
e: The code includes for-sale builders, and the available sources do not quantify their six-digit share.
s5: Stated exit intentions are not completed qualifying transfers.
s5: FMI/CFMA covers broad E&C and includes firms above the target band; 44% of respondents had revenue below $20 million.
s5: Internal transfers qualify only when control actually changes and the operating platform remains transferable.
q: No source measures five-year AI-benefit retention for this industry.
q: Contract mix varies sharply by project complexity, owner sophistication, and negotiated versus bid procurement.
q: The estimate excludes demand-volume effects and implementation costs by design.
d5: The public forecasts cover only 12-18 months, so the five-year extension is judgmental.
d5: Reported spending is nominal and construction-cost inflation is uncertain.
d5: Industrial demand is lumpy and exposed to financing, tariffs, tax credits, permitting, and a few megaprojects.
o: There is no direct measurement of operator-required quantity five years ahead.
o: Factory prefabrication may reduce site labor without eliminating contractor accountability.
o: Large industrial owners may internalize more construction management than the base case assumes.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based revenue from industrial owners under competitively bid or negotiated fixed-price, guaranteed-maximum-price, cost-plus, design-build, and construction-management arrangements; repeat customers and follow-on projects create the repeat-service relationship even though individual projects are finite.
lens.excluded_activities: Warehouses; specialty trades classified outside 236210; owner-captive construction units; shells; non-control financing vehicles; and for-sale/speculative builders whose activity is not an outsourced service to an external customer.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market industrial-building general contractors, design-build firms, and construction-management firms that repeatedly deliver new construction, additions, alterations, maintenance, or repairs for external industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the 2023 occupation and wage mix for broader NAICS 236200, not task-level AI exposure for 236210. I wage-weighted the mix qualitatively: management, project management, estimating, and office work receive materially higher exposure assumptions than field trades, while RICS adoption evidence is used to exclude already-embedded use. This likely overstates substitution if project judgment and site context limit automation, and understates it if multimodal estimating and document workflows mature quickly.
caveats: OEWS is for NAICS 236200 rather than the six-digit industrial-building industry and is 2023 vintage.
The wage-weighted task exposure is a bounded analyst transformation, not an observed automation rate.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and was harmonized across industries, so it may not perfectly represent current industrial-project economics.
a.change_evidence: A 236210 employer time-and-motion study that maps payroll dollars to estimating, submittals, RFIs, scheduling, procurement, safety documentation, accounting, and field execution, paired with audited before/after staffing data from AI deployments.
a.rationale: The broader nonresidential-building workforce is 52.35% construction and extraction by headcount, while management is 15.16%, business and financial operations 12.70%, and office support 7.65%. Higher-paid construction managers (9.30% of employment), project-management specialists (7.25%), and cost estimators (2.29%) concentrate document, scheduling, takeoff, procurement, reporting, and coordination tasks that AI can partly substitute or convert into avoided hiring; most craft execution remains physical and site-specific. RICS found only about 15% active use and 78% no implementation or pilots, leaving most of the modeled opportunity not yet operationally embedded.

d5.bridge: Census measures nominal manufacturing construction spending and AIA forecasts nominal category spending through 2027, not constant-price five-year demand for the exact 236210 service basket. I treat manufacturing construction as the closest demand category, deflate directionally, extend the near-term normalization into a five-year scenario, and allow a high case for renewed plant investment plus alteration and repair. Nominal forecasts overstate real quantity when construction prices rise, while the Census category can omit some industrial structures included in 236210.
caveats: The public forecasts cover only 12-18 months, so the five-year extension is judgmental.
Reported spending is nominal and construction-cost inflation is uncertain.
Industrial demand is lumpy and exposed to financing, tariffs, tax credits, permitting, and a few megaprojects.
d5.change_evidence: A 2027-2031 Dodge, ConstructConnect, FMI, or AIA forecast for US manufacturing-building starts and alterations in constant dollars, segmented by project type and mapped explicitly to NAICS 236210 contractor revenue.
d5.rationale: Manufacturing construction remains high historically but is falling from the 2024 policy-driven peak. Census reported May 2026 manufacturing construction at a $174.8 billion annual rate, 21.9% below May 2025. AIA expects nominal manufacturing spending to fall 11.6% in 2026 after a 6.7% 2025 decline and another 0.6% in 2027. Stabilization, renovations, compliance work, and future capacity cycles support a base above the near-term trough but below current constant-price quantity over five years.

e.bridge: The Census definition establishes that 236210 contains contractor, design-build, construction-management, and for-sale-builder models, while an Ontario ICI contractor survey measures revenue mix rather than the share of US LMM firms meeting the lens. I use the reported 75% repeat-customer work among industrial contractors as evidence that repeat external service is common, then discount for speculative builders, captive-like arrangements, and firms dependent on one-off projects. The Canadian, mixed-contractor sample may over- or understate US 236210 eligibility.
caveats: Repeat-customer revenue share is not the same as the share of firms eligible under the lens.
The contractor survey is Ontario-based and includes trade contractors as well as general contractors.
The code includes for-sale builders, and the available sources do not quantify their six-digit share.
e.change_evidence: A US 236210 firm-level survey or transaction database tagging external-customer revenue, repeat-customer share, speculative development, customer concentration, and standalone management depth for firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
e.rationale: Most firms in the code are organized to deliver construction responsibility for industrial customers, and the Ontario survey found industrial contractors derived 75% of work from repeat customers. The main exclusions are for-sale/speculative builders and firms whose operations are effectively one-project, owner-dependent, or otherwise nontransferable service platforms.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 236210 ($16,152,400,908) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 236210 ($36,987,024,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1414 (margins.json[236]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Homebuilding EBITDA/Sales 14.14%); revenue/employee $492,314 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 236210); class <5: 1,516 firms, avg revenue/firm $936,547 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $132,428 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 530 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,101,130 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $438,500 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 375 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,735,053 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $810,937 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 405 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,817,938 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,519,456 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 98 firms, avg revenue/firm $71,383,347 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,093,605 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 75 firms, avg revenue/firm $234,148,560 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $33,108,606 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 405

caveats: There is no direct measurement of operator-required quantity five years ahead.
Factory prefabrication may reduce site labor without eliminating contractor accountability.
Large industrial owners may internalize more construction management than the base case assumes.
o.change_evidence: Owner procurement data tracking direct self-performance, modular/off-site turnkey supply, autonomous construction, and software-only delivery shares for industrial projects, plus insurer and licensing evidence on whether accountable contractor roles can be displaced.
o.rationale: Industrial-building delivery is a physical, site-specific service with safety, schedule, quality, subcontractor, and contractual accountability. Software can automate design review, takeoffs, documentation, and coordination but cannot by itself assume licensed contractual responsibility, marshal trades and materials, manage site exceptions, or deliver the structure. Some demand can shift to owner self-performance, modular suppliers, or more automated delivery, but nearly all remaining construction quantity should still require an accountable operator.

caveats: No source measures five-year AI-benefit retention for this industry.
Contract mix varies sharply by project complexity, owner sophistication, and negotiated versus bid procurement.
The estimate excludes demand-volume effects and implementation costs by design.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level analysis of a representative 236210 LMM firm's backlog by fixed-price, GMP, cost-plus, and fee basis, combined with bid-win data and gross-margin retention for AI-enabled versus conventional projects through renewal or rebid.
q.rationale: Implemented labor savings can be retained during an existing firm-fixed-price or guaranteed-price project because the contractor bears cost performance, while cost-plus work and open-book construction-management fees pass much of the savings or cost base to the owner. Across renewals and new bids, sophisticated industrial customers, competitive tendering, shared-savings expectations, and lower reimbursable costs progressively reprice part of the benefit. Customer relationships, prequalification, execution risk, and scarce project talent should still preserve a meaningful share of the discounted five-year benefit.

rho.bridge: RICS surveys global built-environment professionals and AGC surveys US commercial contractors about organizational adoption and planned investment, rather than measuring the five-year implemented share of exposed labor opportunity in 236210. I translate low present readiness but rapidly rising investment into a five-year implementation fraction; global RICS results may understate US adoption, while stated plans may overstate completed workflow change.
caveats: RICS respondents are global and only 10% were from the Americas.
AGC reports use or investment intent, not production-scale implementation or labor substitution.
Industrial projects have unusually stringent safety, engineering, owner-specification, and change-control requirements.
rho.change_evidence: Longitudinal 236210 cohort data reporting AI use by workflow, share of projects processed, exception and rework rates, approved governance, and payroll hours avoided over at least two complete project cycles.
rho.rationale: Current implementation is shallow: RICS reports 45% with no implementation, 34% in pilots, about 15% active use, and only a very small share embedded across the organization. Yet AGC's 2026 survey says 61% use AI or plan increased investment, including 45% for office/admin, 23% for estimating, and 20% for design/preconstruction. Over five years, standardized document and preconstruction workflows should move beyond pilots, but fragmented project data, liability, quality control, integration, and scarce AI-skilled staff prevent full implementation of the exposed task pool.

s5.bridge: FMI/CFMA measures stated exit plans among almost 300 engineering-and-construction executives, while Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer plans for US employer-business owners generally. I reduce FMI's 38% planned-exit share for plans that do not close, liquidations, non-control transfers, and transitions outside the exact window, while using Gallup's observed 22% employer plan rate as a cross-check. The E&C executive sample skews larger than the target band, which could raise formal transition activity, but LMM owner concentration could raise succession-driven exits.
caveats: Stated exit intentions are not completed qualifying transfers.
FMI/CFMA covers broad E&C and includes firms above the target band; 44% of respondents had revenue below $20 million.
Internal transfers qualify only when control actually changes and the operating platform remains transferable.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year longitudinal PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, or proprietary broker records for US 236210 firms in the target EBITDA band, linked to ownership age and coded for closed control sale, internal control transfer, closure, and no event.
s5.rationale: FMI/CFMA reports 38% of surveyed E&C owners planned to exit in fewer than five years, but 51% of that group lacked a defined ownership-transfer plan, and most intended internal transitions. Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners planned a sale, transfer, or public offering within five years. A quarter probability recognizes meaningful succession pressure while excluding closures and assuming substantial noncompletion of intentions.

principal_driver: AI-assisted estimating and project administration can expand scarce management capacity while repeat industrial customers still require an accountable builder.
principal_weakness: Manufacturing-project demand is lumpy and currently declining from an unusually elevated peak, while competitive and cost-transparent contracts can reprice savings to owners.
